title: Coffee on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Coffee contains tannins (polyphenolic compounds from roasted beans) that bond to upholstery through hydrogen bonding and pi-stacking interactions. Fresh coffee is water-soluble. Aged stains oxidize and darken — the brown color intensifies as tannin molecules cross-link. Melanoidins from the roasting process add additional brown pigment that is highly resistant to removal.
whyThisWorks: An acidic tannin spotter (professional) or oxidizer (DIY) breaks the chromophore bonds — the colored structures — in the tannin molecules on upholstery. Hydrogen peroxide specifically targets the double bonds that make oxidized tannin brown. The key is avoiding heat and alkaline products, both of which permanently darken tannin stains.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

homeSolutions.instruction: Look for the manufacturer's tag sewn into a seam under a cushion. One letter tells you everything: W = water-safe, S = solvent only (dry cleaning), WS = both okay, X = vacuum only. If it's S or X, stop here and call a pro — water will ring the fabric worse than coffee ever could. If it's W or WS, continue. No tag? Treat as S and call a pro.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden section
homeSolutions.instruction: Flip the cushion or find the back of the frame. Dab a drop of the Dawn + 3% peroxide solution from step 5 and blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If the fabric holds its color, proceed. If dye transfers, stop — upholstery dye is delicate and home peroxide can lighten it permanently. Skip to step 9.

homeSolutions.agent: The tannin rule: no alkaline cleaners
homeSolutions.instruction: Coffee is a tannin stain, just like wine, tea, and berries. Alkaline products — ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, most "all-purpose" upholstery sprays — lock tannin into the fiber permanently and turn a light brown ring into a dark brown one you can't reverse. Use only cold water, Dawn dish soap, 3% peroxide, and white vinegar.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot immediately — never rub, never saturate
homeSolutions.instruction: Press a stack of clean white paper towels or a folded white cloth straight down on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from outside in. Keep going until the cloth stops picking up brown. The critical difference from carpet: foam absorbs liquid fast and hides it — more blotting and less pouring is always right on upholstery.

homeSolutions.agent: Damp-cloth chemistry — never pour on upholstery
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of Dawn with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a bowl. Dip a white cloth, WRING NEARLY DRY, and press onto the stain — outside edges inward. Let it sit 5–10 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Wet the cloth, never the cushion. Repeat the cycle 2–3 times until the stain lifts.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach. On dark or patterned upholstery, skip this step unless the patch test was clean.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar with one cup of cold water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, dab the area. Vinegar neutralizes peroxide residue and keeps any residual tannin in its lighter form as it dries. Blot dry with a fresh clean cloth.

Blot until no more moisture transfers to a dry cloth. Point a room fan at the area for 4–8 hours. Good airflow prevents mildew inside the cushion and prevents water rings from forming at the damp-zone edges. Don't use heat (dryer, hair dryer) — heat sets tannin and can also shrink upholstery fabric.

homeSolutions.agent: Daylight inspect — repeat if shadow remains
homeSolutions.instruction: Inspect under natural light. Coffee rings often look gone when wet and reappear as a pale tan halo when dry. If you see one, repeat steps 5–7 once more. Two rounds is the home ceiling. If a shadow persists, escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional upholstery cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If care code is S or X, the patch test failed, or the stain survived two home rounds, call a pro. Tell them: "Coffee on [W/WS/unknown] upholstery, treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide, vinegar rinse. No alkaline products used, damp-cloth only — no pouring." That handoff lets them go straight to a tannin formula + 6% peroxide + low-moisture extraction.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No over-wetting — causes water rings and backing damage
No chlorine bleach on colored fabric
Always check care code (W, S, WS, X) before treating
fiberSensitivities: Mixed fiber blends common — treat for the most sensitive fiber present
Cushion foam absorbs moisture — avoid saturation
Test in hidden area before treating visible sections

escalation.when: If coffee stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is coffee on upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
